common_name: Cherokee Trail of Tears Bean (Black Snap Pole Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost at the base of a trellis or poles 6–8 feet tall; Cherokee Trail of Tears bean is a black-seeded pole bean variety historically carried by the Cherokee people during their forced removal to Oklahoma in 1838–1839 — one of the most culturally significant American heirloom beans, maintained for nearly two centuries as a living connection to Cherokee agricultural heritage and the tragedy of the Trail of Tears.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost at the base of poles, trellis, or fence 6–8 feet tall; provide support immediately as vines grow rapidly; thin to 4–6 inches apart in rows 24–30 inches apart. Cherokee Trail of Tears bean is one of the most historically significant heirloom varieties in American cultivation — a pole bean carried as seeds by members of the Cherokee Nation during the forced removal from their homelands in Georgia, Tennessee, North Carolina, and Alabama to what is now Oklahoma in 1838–1839, the journey now known as the Trail of Tears in which more than 4,000 Cherokee people died. The black-seeded, purple-streaked pod variety was maintained by Cherokee families after their arrival in Indian Territory and has been preserved as a living memorial to this history, entering mainstream heirloom seed preservation networks through the Seed Savers Exchange in the 20th century. The vigorous vines produce long, slender pods with deep purple to black markings at the snap bean stage; fully mature dry seeds are glossy black and excellent for cooking as a dry bean. The dual snap-and-dry utility, vigorous productivity, and deep cultural history make Cherokee Trail of Tears one of the most important heirloom beans available.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in full sun; flageolet is a French semi-dry bean harvested at the shell bean stage (before pods are fully dry) when the seeds are pale green — the beans retain this delicate green color when cooked, making them the classic French accompaniment to roast leg of lamb and the basis of cassoulet in some regional traditions.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost, 4–6 inches apart in rows 18–24 inches apart; flageolet is harvested at the shell bean stage — when pods are plump and seeds are well-developed but still pale green, before the pods begin to yellow and dry. Flageolet (from the Latin 'flagellum' referring to the flute or pipe shape) is a uniquely French bean variety that occupies the role of the 'shell bean' par excellence in French cuisine — it is neither a fresh snap bean nor a fully dried bean, but is harvested at the intermediate shell-bean stage when the kidney-shaped, pale green to white seeds have developed fully in size and shape but retain their characteristic pale green color and fresh, delicate flavor. The distinctive color persists through cooking when properly prepared (not overcooked), making the finished beans a beautiful pale sage green on the plate — dramatically different from the drab gray-white of common dried beans. Flageolet is most famously the traditional accompaniment to gigot d'agneau (roast leg of lamb) in classic French cuisine, where the delicate flavor of the beans perfectly complements the rich lamb; it is also an important ingredient in traditional cassoulet and flageolets au gratin. Seeds can also be allowed to dry fully for storage and reconstituted, though the characteristic green color is then lost.
sources: French Ministry of Agriculture Heirloom Variety List

common_name: Vermont Cranberry Bean (New England Heirloom Shell Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Vermont Cranberry is an heirloom bush shell bean from New England with a centuries-long tradition of cultivation in Vermont and New Hampshire — the round, red-and-cream speckled beans were the traditional baked bean of New England cooking and remain one of the finest-flavored shell beans for baking, soups, and traditional New England bean dishes.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost; can be harvested as a shell bean at 55–65 days (when pods are plump and mottled red-cream) or allowed to dry fully for 75–90 days for a dry-bean crop; succession sow every 2–3 weeks for continuous shell bean harvest. Vermont Cranberry is a heirloom bush bean with deep New England roots — the speckled red-and-cream oval seeds are the traditional 'cranberry bean' of Vermont and New Hampshire, where they have been grown for baked beans since at least the 1700s. The name 'cranberry bean' (also applied to other speckled beans throughout New England) refers to the red cranberry-like speckled coloration of the pods and seeds at the shell bean stage, not to any relationship with cranberries. The shell beans have a rich, creamy, full flavor that is considered by many cooks superior to navy beans or Great Northern beans for traditional New England baked beans — the beans hold their shape well under long, slow baking while developing a creamy, dense interior texture that absorbs the sweet-savory baked bean flavors. At the dry bean stage the seeds turn darker and lose their red mottling; still excellent for winter cooking. Adapted to short New England growing seasons; performs equally well in Gulf Coast fall gardens.
sources: Vermont Institute of Natural Science Heritage Seed Library

common_name: Zolfino Bean (Italian Pratomagno Yellow Heirloom Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Zolfino is an Italian heirloom bean from the Pratomagno area of Arezzo province in Tuscany, recognized and protected as a Slow Food Presidium — it produces small, round, pale yellow beans with exceptionally thin skin and a uniquely dense, creamy, buttery texture when cooked, considered by many Italian food experts the finest cooking bean in Italy.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in rows 18–24 inches apart after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Zolfino beans are bush type and do not require staking; harvest when pods are dry and papery. Zolfino is an Italian heirloom bean from the Pratomagno hills of Arezzo province in Tuscany, where it has been grown for centuries at elevations between 1,300 and 2,600 feet on the slopes of the Pratomagno massif — the specific local environment (poor, well-drained, slightly acidic volcanic soil with cool nights) contributes to the bean's exceptional quality. Zolfino (the name likely derives from 'zolfo,' Italian for sulfur, referring to the pale yellow color) is a Slow Food Presidium product, meaning it is recognized as an endangered, culturally important food requiring preservation support; only farmers in the Pratomagno area using traditional methods are technically authorized to use the Zolfino denomination. The small (thumbnail-sized), round, pale yellow beans have extraordinarily thin skins — almost imperceptible when eaten — and cook to a dense, creamy, buttery texture entirely different from the grainy or mealy texture of common dried beans; the flavor is rich, earthy, and complex with sweet undertones. Outstanding in simple Italian preparations: olio e fagioli (beans with olive oil), in ribollita soup, or simply simmered with sage, garlic, and good olive oil.
sources: Slow Food Foundation for Biodiversity (Slow Food Presidium)
Italian Ministry of Agriculture Heirloom Variety Registry

common_name: Hidatsa Red Indian Bean (Plains Native American Dry Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Hidatsa Red is a traditional bean of the Hidatsa people of the upper Missouri River valley in North Dakota, traditionally grown as one of the Three Sisters (corn, beans, and squash) in the fertile bottomlands of the Missouri and its tributaries, producing striking dark red, kidney-shaped seeds with excellent rich flavor that made it a staple of Hidatsa cuisine.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost; Hidatsa Red is a bush dry bean that does not require staking; harvest when pods are fully dry and papery, typically 85–100 days from planting. Hidatsa Red is a traditional bean variety of the Hidatsa people (also called Minitari or Gros Ventre), a Native American nation of the upper Missouri River valley in what is now North Dakota, where they cultivated beans, corn, and squash in the fertile bottomlands for centuries before European contact; the bean entered American heirloom preservation networks through ethnobotanical work documenting traditional Plains agriculture and was distributed by the Seed Savers Exchange. The medium-sized, kidney-shaped seeds are an unusual deep brick-red to maroon-red color — distinctive enough to recognize immediately among other dry beans — and cook to a tender, creamy texture with rich, earthy, complex flavor somewhat similar to kidney beans but with more character. In traditional Hidatsa cooking, the beans were dried and stored for winter use, then slow-cooked in stews with dried meat and corn; they are equally excellent in modern preparations — chili, soups, slow-cooked bean dishes, and baked beans where the robust flavor is showcased. Well-adapted to short seasons and the variable conditions of the Northern Plains; good drought tolerance.
